about.label: About
about.heading: Designing and building for real products
about.body: I'm a UX Engineer with a strong focus on building real, production-ready web interfaces. I work across design and frontend development to create clear, usable, and high-performing experiences—from landing pages to full product interfaces. My approach is simple: build fast, keep it clean, and make sure it scales when it needs to.

multirounds.name: Multi Rounds
multirounds.description: Built out of a real need at the table. Multi Rounds lets you run up to four independent timers simultaneously — each with its own Setup, Main, and Extra time phases — so you can manage multiple matches without losing track.

multirounds_page.title: Multi Rounds
multirounds_page.hero_description: A multi-timer web application built for Trading Card Game tournaments and structured time workflows. Run up to four independent timers simultaneously, each with its own phase sequence, configuration, and persistent state.

problem.label: The Problem
problem.heading: Generic timers fail in real tournaments
items: No concept of phases — setup, round, extra time
No support for multiple simultaneous games

idea.label: The Idea
idea.heading: A timer as a sequence of structured phases
idea.description: Instead of a simple countdown, Multi Rounds models each timer as a flow that mirrors how real tournaments actually operate.

items.title: Multi-Timer System
items.desc: Up to 4 fully independent timers running simultaneously. Dynamic layout splits the screen automatically as timers are added.

items.title: Phase-Based Flow
items.desc: Each timer follows Setup → Main → Extra → Idle. Phases transition automatically on completion, mirroring real tournament flow.

items.title: Inline Editing
items.desc: Click to edit the timer name or adjust hours, minutes, and seconds directly — no modal interruptions during live use.

items.title: Persistent State
items.desc: Configuration survives page reloads via localStorage. Each counter stores its own durations and title under a unique key.

items.title: Responsive Layout
items.desc: UI adapts as more timers are added. Font sizes scale with container width to keep readability with 4 timers on screen.

items.title: Single-Screen Model
items.desc: No navigation required. All controls are immediately accessible — designed for fast interaction under pressure.

hook.title: useTcgCounter hook
hook.desc: Each timer is powered by a dedicated React hook that encapsulates state management, countdown logic, phase transitions, and persistence.

persistence.title: Persistence
persistence.desc: Each timer stores its configuration under a unique key with safe fallbacks for malformed data.

items.title: Phase-Based Model
items.desc: Chosen to reflect real-world tournament flow rather than a generic countdown.

items.title: Independent Timer Instances
items.desc: Each timer is isolated to avoid shared state complexity and ensure reliability.

items.title: Inline Editing
items.desc: Removes friction and allows quick adjustments during live matches.

items.title: Local Persistence
items.desc: Avoids login requirements while preserving user configurations between sessions.

challenges.label: Challenges
items: Managing multiple independent timers without state conflicts
Keeping UI readable across dynamic layouts
Designing a flexible yet simple phase system

outcome.label: Outcome
items: Improves clarity and timing consistency during tournaments
Reduces manual overhead for organizers and players
Demonstrates product thinking and UX design under constraints

reflection.label: Reflection
reflection.body: This project started as a personal need and evolved into a focused product. It highlights the importance of building from real use cases, designing for context, and keeping interfaces simple under pressure.
